data.title: In 2026's Wartime Markets, Risky Debt Outshines Treasuries
data.description:
Bonds are prized for offering stability in an asset‑allocation strategy, providing an offset to the higher risk in stocks, particularly during periods of market stress. But since the Iran war began, fixed‑income securities have had a rough ride as markets struggle to assess whether the conflict’s main threat is higher inflation, slower growth (if not recession), or some mix of both.

A broad review of the US bond market, using a set of ETF proxies, shows that some corners of fixed income are posting gains since the conflict began on Feb. 28. But the winners aren’t the usual suspects that you would expect to shine an energy shock and a Middle East war.

The leading performer so far since the fighting started—through yesterday’s close (Apr. 13)—is bank loans. The Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) is up more than 2%, far ahead of the rest of the fixed‑income field. Before the war, few investors thinking about safe havens for a new Middle East conflict would have anticipated that BKLN’s portfolio would become a go‑to port in the storm, but here we are.

BKLN primarily holds floating‑rate senior secured loans—also known as leveraged loans—issued by US corporations (see Morningstar profile below). These loans sit high in the capital structure and typically carry below‑investment‑grade credit ratings. The fund’s relatively strong performance suggests that a preference for higher yield, by way of higher risk, has been a motivating factor for investors during the current turmoil.

A conventional junk‑bond ETF is in second place during the war‑driven rally in select slices of the bond market: the SPDR Bloomberg Short Term High Yield Bond ETF (SJNK) is up 0.6%. Just behind it is the iShares 0–5 Year TIPS Bond ETF (STIP), edging up 0.5%.

Meanwhile, medium‑ and longer‑term Treasuries are underwater so far during the war period. The biggest loser is long‑term government bonds (TLT), which have tumbled 3.8%.

One interpretation: inflation worries are dominating risk perceptions for Treasuries. By contrast, the view that government bonds would benefit from heightened anxiety about slower economic activity or recession seems to be in remission at the moment.

But this explanation isn’t fully satisfying. The Treasury market’s implied inflation forecast has remained relatively stable since the war’s start. The so‑called breakeven rate—the yield spread between nominal and inflation‑indexed Treasuries—hasn’t changed much in recent weeks and is still trading in the mid‑2% range.

If inflation isn’t weighing on Treasuries, what is? Fiscal risk may be a factor. As I’ve been discussing at TMC Research recently, the government’s budget deficit continues to flash warning signs. The high price tag for the war isn’t helping.

Is the bond market looking for new safe havens these days? Hard to know for sure, but the pivot into high‑risk bond securities doesn’t fit neatly into the standard playbook for periods when geopolitical risk spikes.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alphabet: A Powerful Earnings Beat Likely Coming Again (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Previewing Alphabet's Upcoming Earnings Release

Last time I looked at Alphabet (GOOG), in July 2025, the stock was trading at ~$176/share, and I saw a diminishing margin of safety, whereas Microsoft (MSFT), OpenAI (OPENAI), and Anthropic (ANTHRO) seemed to pose some vulnerabilities to its future business development at the time. I rated GOOG as a "Hold", and now I understand that I clearly underestimated Google's AI offering and their balanced business approach of both hardware and software products development.

The company is set to report fiscal Q1 2026 in about two weeks, and based on the data we have on hand, Google is very likely to beat this time, just as it has in recent quarters. More importantly, I see management guiding strongly for calendar 2026, which should lead to above-consensus forward projections. The incoming 'depreciation wave' versus top-line acceleration will be in focus on the earnings call, but given existing operating leverage and GCP momentum, I don't see any serious threats this time. Based on all this, I'm upgrading my previous "Hold" to "Buy" ahead of the earnings print, expecting a continuation of the post-earnings rally as valuation multiples have already shrunk quite meaningfully.

Reasoning For The Upgrade Ahead Of Q1

The reason for my skepticism about Google was about at least partial cannibalization of their high-margin Search business with Gemini (and, of course, other LLMs). The LLMs market was dominated by OpenAI at the time, and I feared that the loss of traffic on the Search side would damage Google's moat (and hence there would be no reason for a higher valuation multiple expectation). It's now clear that I was wrong - OpenAI's market share is dropping, and while Anthropic is the ultimate winner in the enterprise niche (for now, at least), Gemini models are gaining market share in the consumer niche as well. According to AI Multipe data, OpenAI's market share contracted from ~72.5% as of October 2025 to 60.5% as of February 2026 (and I guess right now it's even lower). Gemini's share skyrocketed from less than 14% to almost 24% for the same period.

The "loss of Search" as a fear hasn't materialized - Google's business focus has shifted, but it looks to be for the better. The management commented on the previous call that with the Gemini app scaling to over 700 million MAU, AI Mode queries in Search have proven to be 3x longer than traditional searches:

Queries in AI Mode are 3x longer than traditional searches. We are also seeing sessions become more conversational with a significant portion of queries in AI Mode now leading to a follow-up question.

Source: GOOG's Q4 earningns call

It means that the whole user engagement has become deeper, and hence it now has a better monetization potential for Google in the future periods.

Google is spending tens of billions of dollars on AI infrastructure, but the returns on those investments are actually quite solid so far (they're at least justified). Google Cloud (GCP) revenues accelerated to $17.7 billion in the past quarter (+48% YoY), and this segment's EBIT doubled for the period. The backlog surged by 55% QoQ and amounted to $240 billion, and it's going to be supported by the CAPEX they're spending at the moment. For FY2026, the management guided for $175-185 billion in CAPEX, which is going to be a doubling from FY2025, but the backlog justifies it all, in my view. Most of the invested money will flow to custom silicon (specifically the new Ironwood, TPU7x, chips) that lower energy needs - the ROIC on AI workflows Google will be selling in the next few years, once the hardware optimizations kick in, are going to be much higher than most skeptics might be expecting at the moment. Following the rollout of the more efficient Axion CPUs, Alphabet has already reduced the serving unit cost of Gemini by ~78% over 2025, and it looks to be just the beginning, with more efficiencies from their custom silicon on the horizon.

Now, let me jump to the earnings preview part of my GOOG update and see what's expected. The consensus is waiting for Q1 revenues of $106.89 billion (+18.46% YoY), according to Seeking Alpha, with an adjusted EPS consensus figure set at $2.62 (-6.81% YoY).

When the company reported last time, we saw a double-beating, but the stock price closed almost 3% on the release day because the market feared the earnings regarding depreciation expenses, which were projected to increase meaningfully in Q1.

In terms of expenses, as we've discussed on previous calls, the significant increase in our investments in technical infrastructure will continue to put pressure on the P&L in the form of higher depreciation expense and related data centers operations costs such as energy. In 2025, depreciation increased by nearly $6 billion or 38% from $15.3 billion in 2024 to $21.1 billion in 2025. Given the increase in our CapEx investments in recent years, we expect the growth rate in 2026 depreciation to accelerate in Q1 and meaningfully increase for the full year. We're also planning to continue hiring in key investment areas such as AI and cloud.

Source: GOOG's Q4 earningns call

Keeping in mind FY2026 CAPEX plans of up to $185 billion, I think depreciation could easily surpass $30 billion for the fiscal year 2026, and it's effectively pulling billions in non-cash expenses directly through the firm's P&L. The top line keeps expanding, which is good, but the bottom line remains under pressure for now. At least because of CAPEX, it's not at the level it would have been without it.

Google will have to show its GCP's superior expansion capabilities again, preferably against the backdrop of lower Gemini serving costs - in this case, the operating leverage would be de-risked, and Google's margins will hold steady near 31.6% despite the accounting drag. I expect that some of the $240 billion backlog will convert to revenues, providing a nice buffer for yet another top-line beating. Plus, GCP is very unlikely to lose momentum, driven by the previously mentioned backlog conversion. Also, based on BofA's note to their clients (proprietary source, April 9, 2026), Google's Search traffic is up materially on a YoY basis, and Gemini is still eating away at ChatGPT's market share (for common use purposes, I guess, while Anthropic's Claude is killing it for the enterprises).

BofA analysts added:

For Gemini, traffic and user growth trends remain strong whileGoogle traffic growth was stable y/y, suggesting Claude's traction was incremental or at expense of AI competitors. We continue to see an opportunity for AI integration in Google Search to drive revenue upside through improved intent understanding and better monetization of zero-click queries. Maintain Buy.

What I see as another bullish catalyst that might come right from the upcoming earnings call is the management's wider discussion of the TurboQuant algorithm revealed last month. TurboQuant sent most memory chip manufacturers' stocks to the downside for some time, and while I don't think it's a really huge risk for stocks like SK Hynix or Micron (MU) - see my latest take here - I do believe that for Google, it might be another cost optimization tool that should help them drive up the margins (or make them more stable while they keep spending billions on AI infrastructure projects).

What I also like about Google as it's approaching its Q1 earnings print is the fact that it's trading below its 10-year average P/E ratio on a forward basis, per YCharts data:

Assuming that the majority of CAPEX is spent in 2025-2027, and the past investments start to pay off big way, the margins should pick up eventually and let the EPS soar. Based on the current consensus, which is historically quite pessimistic in Google's case, the firm's EPS should be rising at a 5-year CAGR of ~21.1%. Realistically, Google's EPS should triple in the next 5 years, and the stock is priced at less than 24x FY2027 earnings:

If we assume that GOOG trades at 30x FY2027, with no premium added to the bottom line this time, the stock's fair value arrives at $402.6, which is 26%+ above today's stock price.

Final Words

Of course, there are some clear risks to my today's bullish upgrade on Google. First, given the fact that AI Overviews now appear on 48% of all search queries, and it can summarize any data perfectly, the click-through rates (CTR) fall sharply, so the cannibalization of traffic that I feared last time is actually happening in some form. Second, the CAPEX that the management guided for is truly massive, and if the revenue growth ahead disappoints, we might see a negative FCF ahead - that might send GOOG much lower, because it would look like a sell-off catalyst for some investors.

Despite the risks, I believe that the evolution that Alphabet is going through is quite remarkable, and it should bring in new venues for future growth and development, helping the firm escape the logical maturity phase. I understand the fears around CAPEX and increasing D&A costs, but it's part of the growth phase that any company goes through at some point - Google needs to secure its leading position in the AI market as a whole, and from what I see, they're doing it in great fashion so far. I think GOOG is undervalued by over 26% right ahead of its upcoming Q1 print, so I'm raising my rating from "Hold" to "Buy" this time.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: China Gold Market Update: A Seasonal Demand Rebound In March
data.description:
Looking ahead

Q2 is the traditional off-season for jewellery consumption but a boost may come should the gold price stabilise. Investment demand strength may gain support from declining bond yields and the lack of other local investment opportunities – but the gold price trajectory will remain key to investor decisions. More detailed Q2 outlook and Q1 review will be included in our Gold Demand Trends report coming up on 29 April.

A weak March capped gold’s Q1 gain

Gold plunged in March. The LBMA Gold Price PM in USD fell 12% in the month, weighed by rapidly cooling expectations of future Fed cuts – due to inflationary fears stemming from the Middle East war – and momentum factors as investors unwind their positions in futures, ETFs and options. The SHAUPM saw a similar pullback of 11%, although a depreciating local currency limited the extent of the decline.

A weak March trimmed gold’s Q1 gain. The international gold price in dollars and the Chinese benchmark in RMB both registered a 7% rise in the first quarter (Chart 1). Despite a turbulent quarter for gold, signs of recovery were evident towards the end of March and in early April.

Wholesale gold demand ended Q1 with a seasonal rebound

Banks, jewellers and refiners withdrew 134t of gold from the SGE in March (Chart 2), marking a 57% m/m rebound and a 12% increase y/y. The m/m recovery was largely seasonal, reflecting more working days in March (22 vs. 14 in February) and post–Chinese New Year restocking by industry participants. We believe the gold price pullback also encouraged opportunistic replenishment.

This price dynamic likely contributed to the y/y increase as well, as the sharp gold price rally in March last year had dampened jewellers’ restocking appetite. Nevertheless, the month’s wholesale demand remained below its ten-year average, underscoring continued weakness in the jewellery sector.

A firmer March lifted Q1 wholesale gold demand to 345t – 3% higher y/y but still 23% below the ten-year average. Overall, Chinese gold demand continued to diverge in line with trends observed in 2025: as gold prices surged through most of Q1, strong investment demand offset persistent weakness in gold jewellery consumption.

Chinese gold ETFs saw non-stop inflows throughout Q1

Chinese gold ETFs have now seen inflows for seven months in a row, attracting RMB12bn (US$1.7bn) in March, equivalent to a 8.4t rise in holdings (Chart 3). The plummeting local gold price did not interrupt Chinese investor appetite for gold ETFs. In March, the CSI300 stock index fell 6%, and the local currency depreciated by 0.8% against the dollar; these factors, combined with safe-haven demand prompted by the US-Israel-Iran war and continued regional geopolitical tensions, supported local gold ETF buying. We also witnessed some dip buying during the first half of the month.

Chinese investors bought a total of RMB59bn (US$8.5bn, 50t) of gold ETFs in Q1, shattering the previous quarter’s record. This is the strongest quarter ever: Chinese gold ETFs’ total AUM – supported by the price increase and inflows – rose 26% to RMB304bn (US$44bn) and holdings climbed to 298t, both reaching quarter-end peaks.

Chinese gold futures trading volumes fell by 12% m/m to an average of 443t/day in March. We believe the decline can be largely attributed to lower gold price volatility and a weakening gold price performance, factors that dimmed trader interest (Chart 4). Over the course of Q1, gold futures trading at the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) averaged 468t per day in volumes, well above the five-year average of 265t/day.

The PBoC bought gold on dip in March

The PBoC announced its 17th consecutive monthly gold purchase in March. This 5t addition, the largest since February 2025, pushed China’s official gold holdings to 2,313t (Chart 5). Gold now accounts for 9% of China’s foreign exchange reserves, down from February’s 10% due mainly to the gold price pullback in March. Non-stop buying throughout the quarter has accumulated an additional 7t of gold for the Chinese central bank, the highest since Q1 2025.

Imports rose at the start of the year

China’s gold imports picked up at the beginning of 2026, according to the latest data from China Customs (Chart 6). January net imports reached 77t, a significant increase compared to the net exports of 6t last year. Net imports in February totalled 96t, 63t higher y/y. This robustness was underpinned by resilient demand during the month, and the rebounding local gold price premium also boosted importer interest.

Original Post

data.title: BITO: We Might Only Be Halfway Through Bitcoin's Bear Market
data.description:
I informed investors back in December 2025 that owners of the ProShares Bitcoin ETF (BITO) and bitcoin related investments should consider a strategy shift. My reasoning was that the bull market ended and that we entered a possible year-long bear market from the peak bitcoin price from early October 2025.

BITO did decline about 21% since that article was written. There is some consolation for long-term BITO holders as the total decline when distributions are factored in was 15.4%. My thesis for this article is that bitcoin is only about halfway through the bear market and that another large drop could occur before the bottom is in.

Where We Are In The Bear Market

The reason why I think we are about half of the way through the bear market is because bitcoin's bull and bear markets have been consistent in length. Each price cycle since Bitcoin's conception ended in bear markets that lasted approximately one year. This is followed by a bull market, which typically lasts for about three years.

Since we are currently in the seventh month of the bear market, this bear market could end approximately in September 2026. The bear market could be a little shorter or a bit longer, but one-year is a good approximation based on the past trends.

Bitcoin's Weekly Chart

Bitcoin's weekly chart above shows two bear flag formations since the price peaked in October 2025. The current bear flag should result in another significant drop, perhaps down to the $40Ks. The previous two declines since the peak price were significant at about 36% and 39%. So, the next drop could take us into the $40Ks if we get a similar drop. Of course, this depends on what price the decline occurs from.

Bitcoin's price could rise to about $80K within the flag formation of this bear flag. The price did recently recover from an oversold condition according to the RSI indicator. The MACD looks like it is about to cross above the red signal line. So, we could see a mini rally up to about $80K. However, given the length of bitcoin's bear markets, I would expect the stock to make another significant drop before the bottom is in.

If we look back at the previous price cycle, bitcoin experienced three bear flags before the price recovered back to a bull market. So, I think another bear flag is likely to form within the next couple of months. The previous price cycle bottomed after forming a bullish divergence between the RSI and bitcoin's price. The bullish divergence occurs when the price makes a lower low, while the RSI makes a higher low.

Bitcoin could be setting up a similar situation. This would occur if the price makes another low (below the $60K level), while the RSI makes a higher low above the 26 to 27 level. If a bullish divergence forms, it could indicate the bottom for this current price cycle.

Strategy For The Bear Market's 2nd Half

For those investors who don't wish to short bitcoin, you could consider dollar-cost averaging into BITO, another bitcoin-related ETF, or into bitcoin itself. This would take the guesswork out of trying to time the bottom. Investors could buy BITO or a bitcoin ETF of their choice at regular intervals (i.e., every week or every month).

This will allow you to build your position while benefitting from the lower prices as compared to the peak price from early October 2025. By dollar cost averaging into bitcoin or BITO now, you are likely to benefit from the bull market which may begin around September or October 2026. That estimation is based on the consistency of bitcoin's bear markets which I discussed earlier.

For those who are comfortable with shorting bitcoin, an interesting ETF is the YieldMax MSTR Short Option Income Strategy ETF (WNTR). This is a short play on Strategy (MSTR) which is highly levered to bitcoin. Strategy declined about 90% in the last bitcoin bear market. So, a similar decline could occur during this bear market. WNTR pays a generous weekly distribution. The price doesn't even need to rise much, because the benefit comes from the weekly payouts. WNTR currently has a trailing 12 month yield of 97%.

The chart above shows the daily chart for WNTR as compared to bitcoin, IBIT, and BITO. The chart was adjusted for distribution payments. It is clear that WNTR significantly outperformed the long bitcoin plays since the October 2025 peak BTC-USD price.

My expectations are that WNTR will continue to outperform the long bitcoin plays until about September 2026. Of course, there are likely to be some short-term fluctuations in price. Bitcoin does tend to have small rallies within its bear markets. The next significant leg down for bitcoin could occur any time now. I'm basing this on the consistency of the patterns of bitcoin's four-year price cycles. This time will probably have similar patterns to the past price action.

Risks to the Investment Thesis

It is possible that the bear market for bitcoin is much shorter than it was in the past price cycles. There is no guarantee that this bear market will last for about one year as they did in past cycles. Bitcoin could begin the next bull market at any time which would prove my thesis to be incorrect.

This uncertainty makes timing the market much more risky. So, some investors may prefer to hold or dollar cost average into bitcoin to reduce risk.

Final Thoughts

I'm sticking with my thesis that bitcoin's bear market will continue to about September 2026. As a result, I will continue to hold WNTR and look to shift to a bitcoin-related long position around that time. If something happens to invalidate my thesis, then I'll consider making the shift sooner.

The good news for bitcoin holders is that the bear markets have tended to make less percentage declines in each price cycle. With the last bear decline being about 78% in the last price cycle, we could experience a smaller percentage decline this time—perhaps a 70% to 75% decline. This would take the price down to about $31,500 to $38,000. Of course the total percentage decline could be more or less than that. Time will tell.

data.title: Rocket Lab: This Pullback Is A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
I'm Still Bullish On RKLB

Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) is an interesting space stock that has managed to soar by over 500% since my 2024 bullish call, mainly thanks to the management's execution and the multiple expansions that the market participants started to grant it for better prospects ahead. However, since I updated my Buy rating on RKLB in January 2026, the stock has fallen by over 18%, massively underperforming the general U.S. stock market.

I can't say that the selloff was triggered for no reason at all—the delay in the Neutron launch and the market's shifting attention to the upcoming SpaceX (SPACE) IPO are likely pressing the stock. Plus, there was an ATM program that was also adding to the supply of shares. Despite that, if we zoom out and try to understand the firm's longer-term opportunity in the niche it's exploring, I think we'll see that the stock isn't that expensive.

On most conventional metrics, RKLB can't be called cheap. I agree with that view. But if we adjust for the growth factor, the current valuation setup would look relatively reasonable, in my opinion. Based on all this, I think RKLB still deserves a Buy rating, especially given the bullish catalysts that I see in the medium term.

Why Do I Think So?

As most of Rocket Lab's investors and followers know, in January 2026, the firm had a major setback when the Stage 1 tank of its new medium-lift Neutron rocket ruptured during a hydrostatic pressure test. It's a manageable thing, in my understanding, as I saw no major breakdown that would have changed the perception of investors (and the company itself) regarding the viability of their tech. But as a logical result of what happened, RKLB had to push the target for Neutron's flight to Q4 2026. I guess they decided to play it safe and make sure these kinds of failures don't emerge again when they near the launch time, and that's why we saw Q4 and not Q2/Q3 2026. Anyway, this delay spooked some growth investors who saw an execution problem here.

I don't see any meaningful management execution risks in RKLB's growth story. There are always things to be aware of, but we have to remember that in 2025, RKLB delivered 21 Electron launches, making it the world's most frequently launched orbital small rocket. The launch segment has become gross-profitable, meaning that the launch revenues exceed COGS—it's the main goal for any hyper-growth company trying to prove their business model actually works.

In the past, the management promised they'd build an end-to-end space company by acquiring and scaling critical subsystem suppliers, and they did. First, they bought and successfully integrated Sinclair Interplanetary (focused on star trackers/reaction wheels) and then SolAero (solar arrays). Then, in early 2026, we saw them buying Optical Support and Precision Components Limited (precision machining and optics focus), and getting regulatory approval to acquire Mynaric (laser optical communication).

With over $1.3 billion in SDA contracts already secured (see above), I believe we'll see that in 2026 and 2027, revenues from these massive satellite manufacturing projects will heavily hit RKLB's income statement.

So, the past isn't always telling what's coming next, but from the past M&A activity and the profitability trajectories after all the deals done, I think the management's execution track is more than solid. Once the newest deals get fully integrated as well, I expect to see more fixed-cost control and hence higher GP margins (probably a quicker path to the net bottom line profitability on an adjusted basis).

Another thing that has been pressing the stock price, as I mentioned above, was the $1 billion ATM program—for a $35-40 billion market cap company, it's actually a lot of capital issued that way. Anyway, this program has already been finished. On April 8, the firm announced the ATM completion, having sold over 6.4 million shares into the market for ~$474 million in gross proceeds (another ~7.4 million shares were collared, meaning that immediate selling pressure from the banks hedging their positions has likely already occurred). In other words, the heaviest selling pressure from the ATM is likely over now that the program is complete—the supply side has gotten a relief factor that now doesn't limit the stock's appreciation (if the bullish catalysts/news are present).

I expect from Rocket Lab a successful launch of Neutron in late 2026, and if there are no failures, it's going to finally open a large addressable market for the firm, unlocking billions in potential defense contracts from the NSSL (National Security Space Launch) Lane 1 program. By the same time, if SpaceX completes its IPO preparation and we indeed see the targeted valuation close to a $2 trillion market cap, it's going to become a major repricing factor for RKLB, as it's going to be the only other publicly investable peer with proven medium-lift reusability.

We can't buy RKLB cheaply, it's true. For example, if we take a look at the firm's forward sales, in FY2027, the implied price-to-sales comes in at ~32x, which is a lot compared to almost anything else on the market right now. However, we see that 2027 is only the beginning for RKLB—from 2027 to 2034, the revenue CAGR is going to be over 29%, according to the same consensus.

RKLB is a very long-duration investment, meaning that it's going to take years before it becomes reasonably valued. But there's a very important thing here: if Rocket Lab secures more contracts after the Neutron launch and all other initiatives progress well, the current consensus estimates are going to move up significantly. It's common practice, and we've seen it already happening with RKLB since late 2024:

Assuming the same kind of revision strength ahead, it's not difficult to foresee a reasonable 10x P/S ratio arriving at a quicker-than-projected pace.

I don't have a strict price target for RKLB in mind, but based on Wall Street's consensus PTs, the stock might have an upside potential of almost 23%. I'll take it as a landmark.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

While I'm still quite optimistic regarding Rocket Lab's prospects, the company must prove it can scale its revenues to the consensus range by 2032. It's still more of a "show me" growth story for the next few years at a minimum, and it's not a suitable investment for value-minded or risk-averse investors.

Also, as I noted in my January update, if we see another failure during the maiden flight later this year, the stock will likely contract dramatically because the market will start to reprice the company's future contracts and backlog expansion to the downside. Also, we have to keep in mind that RKLB competes with SpaceX, which has better pricing power thanks to its scale.

The Bottom Line

I'm still bullish on RKLB after its post-January selloff, as I see some of the negative triggers fading away in the coming months. The growth story and the potential expansion look promising to me, and the repricing potential is still there—once SpaceX goes public, it's going to become a major bullish trigger for RKLB, in my view. Based on all this, I'm reiterating my Buy on RKLB today.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: 2 Dividend Growth Stocks Down Double-Digits With Massive Upside Potential
data.description:
Introduction

Buy when others are fearful. This is something Warren Buffett has famously said. Of course, this was in reference to quality stocks that are being mis-priced by the market.

In the case of the two dividend growth stocks I discuss today, I believe it may be the case. Both have been impacted by global trade uncertainty, high inflation, and a seemingly resilient, but weakening economy.

While both are likely to underperform for the remainder of 2026, I think they can see strong returns once we see more economic clarity, likely in the next 12 months. While I'm not necessarily saying to buy these two stocks right now as they are likely to see more volatility, there are worth putting on your radar.

In this article, I discuss these two, high growth dividend stocks, their fundamentals, and why they both could see massive upside potential in the not too distant future.

Market Uncertainty

Although we are seeing a lot of uncertainty right now, at the time of writing President Trump has suspended his previous deadline of attacking Iran for 2 weeks, hoping the two sides can come to an agreement.

While stocks jumped as a result, I believe we will see more market volatility. A lot can change in the next 8 months, but it seems like the S&P (SP500) could be headed for less than stellar returns.

While some stocks will still perform well, the two companies I discuss are also likely to see muted returns. One, due to their sectors and two, because of inflation headwinds.

But this could be viewed as a long-term buying opportunity. Especially, if you love growing dividends with upside potential like I do. Without further ado, let's get into these two stocks.

#1 T-Mobile US (TMUS)

In recent years, T-Mobile has established themselves as a force in the telecom space, significantly outperforming peers AT&T (T) and Verizon (VZ) in the past decade. They've even managed to outperform the S&P, up 417% compared to 218%.

But in the past year, the tables have turned with both peers in the green and TMUS down more than 18% at the time of writing. In my opinion, this had to do with the strong price appreciation the telecom giant saw in 2024.

They also established a dividend at the end of 2023. And has since grown it by an impressive 57% from $0.65 to $1.02 a share. Their last increase was a 16% raise from $0.88.

While I do expect strong double-digit growth from TMUS going forward, short-term volatility is likely to result in price underperformance. In their most recent quarter, TMUS missed analysts' estimates by $0.18.

They suffered a severance charge of $293 million, resulting in EPS declining 27% from $2.57 the prior year. EPS amounted to $1.88. But they still managed to see bottom line growth from $9.66 to $9.72.

Revenue grew from $81.4 billion to $88.3 billion. Growth is expected to pick up this year to 6.30%. But for 2027 and 2028, analysts expect TMUS to see strong, double-digit growth again.

And this will likely be led by management's expectations to see strong subscriber growth and free cash flow growth.

For 2025, they ended the dividend with a safe payout ratio of 23%. They paid out $4.1 billion in dividends and returned $13.9 billion to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks.

For their upcoming quarter, management stated they plan to repurchase approximately $5 billion worth of shares due to share price weakness. And for 2026, I expect shareholder returns to be much higher as a result.

Their balance sheet was solid with leverage at 2.4x, below AT&T's 2.68x, and slightly above Verizon's 2.3x. Net debt stood at $84 billion, while total debt was $88.6 billion at the end of Q4.

Currently, T-Mobile trades at $196 a share, giving them a forward P/E of 17.58x using earnings estimates. This is below their 5-year average of 32.77x. Even if the stock has re-rated from above 30x earnings, they still trade below their fair value multiple of 24.51x.

Seeing how the company has performed and their growth expectations, I think a 20x - 25x earnings multiple is appropriate. This gives investors massive upside potential to their end of 2028 price target of $400.

#2 Badger Meter Inc (BMI)

Badger Meter Inc is a company that seems to get little coverage. However, the stock is a quality, dividend growth stocks with 33 years of consecutive dividend raises under its belt.

They were founded in 1905 and are a water solutions provider. They're responsible for creating the first frost-proof water meter. And help customers optimize operations by offering hand-held devices, communications, data, visuals, and measurement hardware.

Here's a list of some of their products for those unfamiliar with the company. But you can see from the chart below, the stock has also underperformed, down 12% in the past year. In my opinion, part of this was due to their gross overvaluation where they traded at more than 50x earnings.

Since, the stock has fallen from close to $260 a share to $152 where they currently trade. I also believe macro uncertainty resulted in their overvaluation. Reasons for this are their strong fundamentals, high dividend growth, and debt-free balance sheet.

Investors were likely looking for stocks backed by quality fundamentals that can also offer income. And finding a quality wealth compounder with no debt is quite rare.

BMI has also seen slower top and bottom line growth. In their latest quarter at the end of January, revenue declined from the prior three quarters. It declined from $236 million in Q3, $235 million in Q2, and $222 million in Q1.

EPS growth was also stagnant at $1.14. Although it managed to beat analysts' estimates, it was down $0.05 from the prior quarter, $0.03 from Q2, and 12.3% from the first quarter.

But EPS still managed to increase $0.10 from the prior year's quarter. And revenue grew year-over-year from $205.2 million to $221 million. For the entire year, revenue grew 11%, while their bottom line increased 13.2% from $4.23 to $4.79 a share.

So, despite headwinds, BMI still managed to see double-digit growth in a challenging environment. The company has been impacted by global trade volatility due to tariffs, which has resulted in higher expenses.

These include copper. Project pacing and fewer operating days also resulted in a 6% decline in utility water sales. But the company's dividend was backed by strong cash flows.

Cash from operations grew from $155 million to $183.7 million year-over-year, and this was despite capital expenditures increasing $1.2 million. These amounted to $14 million for 2025.

BMI's capital light business is also something I like about the company aside from their debt-free balance sheet. Free cash flow grew from $142 million to $170 million.

BMI only paid out $43.5 million in dividends, giving them plenty of room for future double-digit increases with a 25.5% payout ratio. They also frequently repurchase shares and upped their current program by $75 million, bringing the total to $150 million.

This is expected to be depleted through 2028. In Q4, they bought back $15 million worth of shares. And for 2026, I expect management to take advantage of the suppressed share price.

You can also see that like TMUS, growth is expected to pick up in 2027.

BMI reports earnings this month. And you can see they've seen 6 downward revisions. For 2026, I expect them to see more volatility and growth suppression due to macro uncertainty.

But on a positive note, EPS and revenue are expected to increase from Q4. I expect EPS in a range of $1.20 - $1.26 and revenue between $230 million to $233 million.

Using their earnings estimates, Badger Meter still appears expensive around 30x earnings. This is well-below their 5-year average around 46x. If they return to their normal multiple around 35x, their upside potential is massive with a price target of $227 by the end of 2028.

Risks & Investor Takeaway

Both of these stocks face risks from the ongoing war. The longer it goes on, the likelihood of both stocks continuing to underperform. TMUS could see slower subscriber growth as recession risks rise.

For Badger Meter, if they continue to see lower growth, it's possible for shares to re-rate lower as they still appear expensive around 30x earnings. Especially, if higher inflation impacts their projects expected to come online and drive growth.

And their higher growth expected for 2027 could be muted due to recession risks. But their debt-free balance sheet gives the company flexibility to make acquisitions to drive future growth.

While I do expect both companies to see volatility, long-term I suspect both can return to high growth and see strong upside potential in the next 12 to 36 months. And due to their underperformance in the past year, both could be considerations inside a dividend growth portfolio.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: A Case Of Misplaced Fears
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I’ve often found that the companies that I enjoy writing about the most are the ones with the most 50/50 division in terms of sentiment. Blue Owl Capital (OWL) seems to fit this quite well, with bulls arguing that the sell-off has gone too far and bears arguing that its portfolio is going to collapse because of the AI revolution. In rough terms, that is the perception I have of the two sides.

The title of the article, I think, makes it blatantly obvious where my views are on this. I’m not convinced that these fears hold that much weight. It covers a moderate portion of the portfolio, but not something that would cripple it and cause a dividend cut. Certainly not the valuation decline we’ve seen over the past 6 months. But it’s only really a reflection of where the market sentiment is right now.

Not that long ago I wrote about Adobe (ADBE), which suffers the same at the moment, even though its actual financial figures show momentum. When the dust settles and the market realizes the fears that AI would replace everything, these valuations are going to correct upwards, and I’m here to take advantage of that before it happens. As the cherry on top with OWL, I also get a near 11% yield while doing so. Rating shares a Strong Buy.

Credit, Strategic Capital And Real Estate - The Business Of Blue Owl

As of the end of 2025 OWL held over $300 billion in assets under management. A clear majority of this is in “Credit,” which is also the “legacy” part of the business. This is the main business driver, and direct lending is leading the charge here at $115 billion AUM. Out of this, $65.3 billion is fee-paying AUM, or FPAUM. This is also what most of the bears will be pointing to as for why OWL will perform poorly as an investment because it concerns the private equity buyouts that the company engages in.

The 16.2% rise in the value of credit lending was reportedly large, driven by $20.7 billion raised in new capital, $13 billion of which was for direct lending. So this part alone managed to raise Credit AUM by 9.5%. I’d say that is pretty solid. Since the ratio of AUM and FPAUM is 56.7% for direct lending, it adds $7.3 billion in new fee-paying assets. Overall, though, I’d say there are some valid criticisms, especially that the FPAUM actually climbed at a lower rate YoY compared to AUM here, 9.4% compared to 9.5%. It’s barely material, but still. If it continues, it would impact the ROA.

Even though some point to credit being a main risk for the business, it’s currently showing a pretty massive operating leverage where fee-related revenues “FRE” are growing at a faster pace than AUM. This is the definition of operating leverage.

For the year ended December 31, 2025, approximately 85% of our management fees were earned on AUM that we refer to as Permanent Capital.

The second part of OWL is the GP Strategic Capital, which covers $69.1 billion in AUM. I’d argue that this is the MOAT of the company right now because of the permanent capital. This references equity stakes that OWL buys in other PE firms. As a result, it collects a management fee perpetually. In this segment OWL reported only $1.1 billion as AUM not yet paying fees. This means that almost all of the capital here goes towards reinvestments to build up the $39.5 billion FPAUM even more. The ratio of non-utilized AUM to AUM is 0.015x. This means OWL is squeezing every bit of available capital to raise revenues.

I think this table is the perfect example of why exactly the AUM in GP Strategic Capital is at little risk of declining. The accumulated return for investors placing capital here has been 22.8% on a blended average. That beats most average investor returns by a decent margin. With that performance, who would reallocate? Not many, I would guess.

Looking at management fees, OWL reported $636 million here in 2025, up 3.4% YoY. It’s a top-line revenue and doesn't account for opex. In 2025 OWL ended with an FRE margin of 58.3%, which implies that GP Strategic Capital on its own is bringing in $370.7 million. Why this matters is because OWL has over the past 12 months paid out $546 million in dividends and bought back $53 million worth of Class A shares, for a combined $599 million. The point being that this segment runs very little risk of seeing outflows. It might not grow at the same pace as Credit, but it is a massive cash cow right now, covering 61.8% of the last 12 months shareholder returns.

The last part to cover is the real estate assets that OWL manages. AUM sits at $80.6 billion, with FPAUM at $48.8 billion, leading to a conversion ratio of 60.5% right now - slightly higher than the credit ratio and above GP Strategic Capital as well (57.1%). Some might argue that this is another risky part of OWL because of the digital infrastructure assets here, concerning data centers. Companies like Oracle (ORCL) are seeing lower credit ratings because of the sheer amount of debt they are taking on to handle this rise in AI investments. If this is a trend that continues, the AUM OWL holds here might be under increased risk of defaults. Representing 5.5% of the entire portfolio, I don’t think the risk is detrimental. It won’t wipe out OWL overnight if its clients all default.

Oracle has confirmed a continued rise in data center deals, with the value of its lease commitments totalling $248 billion as of November 30, 2025.

It has been the area where OWL has grown the most, and by pulling out of a $10 billion deal with Oracle, I think it has improved the portfolio. ORCL was already heavy in debt, and it would have added unnecessary default risk to the segment. Considering ORCL has lease commitments of nearly $250 billion, more than 10x what it reported in TTM operating cash flows, OWL dodged a big bullet here, I think. Why the stock has slid so much seems to relate more to the market assuming OWLs growth engine is slowing down. As I’ve shown up until this point, that just isn’t the case right now.

Management fees in Rreal assetshave risen sharply here at 99.3% YoY. Part of the reason was the acquisition of IPI ending in Q1 2025. YoY the AUM here climbed 63.4%. This again showcases a serious amount of operating leverage for the company. When measured with the FRE net margin of 58.3%, Real Assets netted OWL $218.1 million.

The consolidated figure here from the table when allocating the same 58.3% FRE margin for all segments was $1.447 billion, slightly below the actual $1.496 billion reported. This is an increase of 37% YoY. At the same time, the management fees grew by 24.3% instead. Those who have gone over the 10-K might recall that the FRE margin dropped slightly from 59.4% to 58.3% between 2024 and 2025. Despite this, though, OWL has shown it’s able to tackle this by raising more capital and increasing its combined AUM to offset this loss.

The biggest change I came across when actually looking at all three segments is how GP Strategic Capital is now at a much larger weight in terms of overall FRE. I can point back to how this segment has, at the moment, very sticky fee generation due to the strong performance reported, reducing the likelihood of fund outflows. It’s also where the main bearish argument falls apart because OWL is actively reducing the weight of credit and focusing on these cash machines instead.

The last point here is that with those two segments, they generate a combined FRE of $589.3 million. Because this figure is incredibly stable, it’s fair to assume a similar amount will come this year too. This covers 98.3% of the TTM dividends and buybacks that OWL has performed. Again, this ensures that a yield of above 10% can continue but is also sustainable in the long term.

Will AI Disruption Render Borrowers Insolvent?

The story right now with OWL seems to be about whether or not it can continue to grow its portfolio while mitigating outflows of funds. News sentiment isn’t the most bullish right now, with most articles seemingly questioning the company's survival even. But the people who have the best “finger on the pulse” are banks and lenders. The risk assessments they make tell a much better story about the current market condition than any article could.

Outstanding debt climbed to $3.369 billion, up from $2.639 billion. This also raised the cost of debt for OWL to 4.86%. Normally you’d say that this is a risk and perhaps the start of something more. The rise came from a rise in the Revolving Credit Facility. It increased 42% to $2.45 billion with the opportunity of climbing to $3 billion if certain thresholds are met.

The borrowing rates for balances outstanding under the Revolving Credit Facility as of December 31, 2025 and December 31, 2024 were 4.94% and 5.72%, respectively

It was then also extended by 5 years to August 8, 2030, and the effective rate declined to 4.94%. This sort of thing doesn't happen to a company that is supposedly struggling or would have its entire business model turned upside down because of investor withdrawals.

But despite the institutional view seemingly being that OWL is doing just fine and that the portfolio performance in 2025 showing the same, market sentiment is different. $20 billion in outflows from private credit funds is not something to shrug off, but the scale just isn’t enough to swing the bull case.

Looking At Blue Owl Technology Finance Tells A Different Story

Because OWL is the manager of the actual loans, the loan portfolio is found in the managed companies, like Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp. The last 10-K shows that exposure to software companies is a combined 31.5% (application & systems), another 13.9% to healthcare technology. There is no major overexposure to any given company, with the 2 largest being Squarespace and Databricks, both with a combined value of about $120 million. This is peanuts for a company with AUM of over $300 billion. Portfolio yield might have fallen to 8.8% from 9.4%, but it’s still very profitable.

The argument that OWL might face lower earnings because companies like Squarespace or Databricks would succumb to AI disruptions holds no serious weight when you actually look at the portfolio it manages. The reality is that OWL is incredibly well diversified at the moment and well protected against industry downturns.

Besides, almost all of the loans are first-lien senior secured, meaning OWL will be first in line to recover value should bankruptcy happen. The $14.2 billion portfolio has 76.8% first-lien loans, calculated using the fair value. The interest rate paid on the portfolio was 6% in 2025, a slight decline from 6.1% in 2024. This shows a net interest spread of 2.8% when weighted against the portfolio yield.

Sure, it’s down from 3.3% in 2024, but the pipeline has shifted. The “portfolio” more than doubled principal debt to $6.3 billion because of the increased lending potential to software companies in the AI boom. There is also $1.8 billion in unfunded commitments, which, at 8.8%, is an additional $158.4 million in interest income. At 2.8% net interest spread, this is closer to $50.4 million in pure net interest income. Again, this helps shore up the dividend and buybacks even more.

The Full Picture

Blue Owl Capital is the managing company of a whole array of different lenders. It has managed to rapidly raise its AUM in a short period of time. With the boom in AI investments, it has managed to secure considerable loans with major companies. But because of the fears that AI will then replace these companies (the borrowers), leading to massive defaults, the stock price of OWL has fallen sharply.

Now at nearly 11% FWD yield, the time couldn't be better to add or start a position. A more sober look at the actual portfolio exposure shows that much of the core business is protected against these fears. The cash machine that is GP Strategic Capital and growth in Real Assets alone ensure the dividend distributions. The doomsayers saying the Credit Line will collapse haven't looked at the underlying exposure, which is both well diversified and protected with first-lien loan types. It also boasts a healthy portfolio yield and a moderate cost of debt at 6%, for Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp, that is. The best gauge is to see whether the fears for OWL are justified or not. In my view, they are misplaced, leading to my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: VTEB: Hold The Best-In-Class Muni-Vehicle With Currently Capped Upside
data.description:
The Vanguard Tax-Exempt Bond ETF (VTEB) ETF seeks to track the performance of a benchmark index that measures the investment-grade segment of the U.S. municipal bond market.

VTEB is a cheap and liquid passive vehicle in the national investment-grade Muni space. The asset class is the problem currently. With sticky inflation, a fed in deliberate pause, and the long end of the treasury curve near 5% (and stuck there), I do not see a path for VTEB to outperform the broad fixed income benchmark by any meaningful margin. There is a potential for carry (as real yields are still positive), but no capital appreciation in sight.

VTEB is being initiated on Seeking Alpha as a hold, due to the sticky environment and muni performance that is not expected to wildly outperform the broader asset class.

The Hold

The portfolio of VTEB has 9,771 holdings. The 30-day SEC yield is ~3.46%, which is about 5.85% for an after-tax yield at the top federal tax bracket, plus the 3.8% NIIT. The vehicle itself is excellent. The macro setup currently is the main constraint, with one expected fed rate cut in 2026, and possibly none, with core CPI being sticky within the mid 2% range, and the long end of the T curve ranging between 4.75%-5.00%. The US30Y closed the week near 4.92%, with the US10Y near 4.34%. Fed funds futures additionally imply no further easing from the current levels, which is consistent with what the FOMC speakers are commenting on, particularly within the March SEP. The Fed is in a deliberate pause, and commodities inflation pressure consistent with ongoing geopolitical pressure makes the threshold to which to fed feels compelled to cut rates higher.

For a muni fund with about 7 years of average duration, this environment translates to clipping coupons with no real rate tailwind. A 25-bps parallel rally would carry about 175 bps of price appreciation, while a 25-bps selloff erases any carry from holding. The expected return is parallel to the SEC yield, and the yield is OK, but nothing to write home about. This is the main reason is earns a hold from me.

Tradability

The average daily volume runs about 8 million shares, or about $400mm a day. There is a tight bid-ask for this ETF, which is a win, considering the muni market is illiquid by nature. AUM of the fund is approx. $42B and has net inflows of about $5.8B over the past year.

Construction wise, VTEP tracks the S&P National AMT-Free Municipal Bond Index. AA-rated paper comprises about 55-60% of the fund, and AAA takes about 20%, which leaves some contained exposure to A rated and below. This index also excludes tobacco bonds, housing bonds, and conduit issues, which separates it from broader competitors. Around 33% of the assets sit in GO bonds, with the remainder in revenue bonds in transportation, utilities, education, and healthcare. This portfolio has geographical concentration within the largest issuing states, which is something to consider, but that is also just how the muni market works, as a low population state like South Dakota will not issue as much debt as a larger population state like New York or California (NY, CA, and TX account for 30-40% of assets combined in the fund).

Top 10 Holdings

The top 10 positions are tiny in absolute terms because the index is genuinely diversified across nearly 10,000 holdings. The largest single bond holding is 12 basis points. Total top 10 weight is 1.52%.

# Holding Weight
1 Vanguard Municipal Cash Management Fund 0.58%
2 Metropolitan Transportation Authority 5% 02/01/2028 0.12%
3 California, State of 5.25% 08/01/2032 0.11%
4 South Carolina Association of Governmental Organizations 5% 03/02/2026 0.11%
5 State of Colorado 5% 06/30/2026 0.11%
6 JP Morgan Chase Putters/Drivers Trust 02/10/2033 0.11%
7 Regents of the University of California 5% 05/15/2036 0.10%
8 New York City Transitional Finance Authority 5% 11/01/2033 0.09%
9 Dallas Independent School District 5% 02/15/2056 0.09%
10 Dormitory Authority of the State of New York 4% 02/15/2047 0.09%

The largest allocation is into the Vanguard Municipal Cash Management Fund. This is the sweep vehicle for unsettled cash and intraday liquidity, which is needed, but it does mean about 0.58% of the fund is sitting in something floating rate versus fixed-rate bonds. Additionally, the MTA position the largest individual bond at a AA-rated essential service provider. After this, you have the JPM Putters/Drivers trust, which is a tender-option bond structure (floating rate, leveraged muni vehicle that adds some nice structural complexity to the funds holding) which is a nice add at a small 0.11% exposure to an already well diversified portfolio. The remaining 9,761 bonds in the portfolio are where the main exposure to the market is. The holdings lean towards state GO debt, transportation services, public universities, school districts, and some water/sewer revenue bonds. Credit quality is high overall, and the default rate is historically low.

Based on the February 2026 muni update by GS, muni-to-treasury ratios at the end of February were around 59% for 5Y, 63% for 10Y, and 88% for 30Y. The front and intermediate parts of the muni curve were rich vs U.S. treasuries, and only the long end of the yield curve offered fair and attractive value. Loomis Sayles came to a similar conclusion in their own q1 muni update, and called intermediate ratios fairly rich by historical trends.

The average duration of 7 years in VTEB puts it within the intermediate zone where the muni ratios are the richest currently. As of now, this is unfortunately the worst part of the curve from a TEY (tax equiv. yield) view. A muni investor within the highest federal tax bracket sits at about 5.85% on VTEB instead of a 4.34% yield on a Treasury. The 150-bps point pickup is real, but it is the smallest the muni curve has offered in years for this duration. Muni exposure is more attractive towards the long end of the curve where the ratios are closer to the historical fair value, instead of the intermediate index that VTEB tracks.

After Tax Yields – And what it means for VTEB

The 3.46% yield understates what Muni delivers to a taxable investor, sometimes by a meaningful margin. A TEY can be calculated by the following: TEY = Muni yield / 1 – marginal tax rate). For an investor in the top federal bracket of 37%, plus the 3.8% net investment income tax of 3.8% (Tax that kicks in above $200k single / $250k joint), the effective federal rate on taxable bond income is 40.8%. That gives a TEY of about 5.85%. This is the number that an investor should use to compare against a taxable alternative. With a US10Y yield of 4.34%, the muni option picks up ~ 1.51% of after-tax yield, and versus an IG corp bond of about 5.0%, you pick up 85 bps with a materially lower risk profile.

The catch with this is that the 5.85% TEY assumes you are capturing the full federal and state exemption on every bond in the portfolio. VTEB does not deliver this. Muni interest is federally exempt no matter the state, but the state tax exemption only flows through if you live in the state that issued the paper. A national muni fund like VTEB has bonds spread over all 50 states, which means a top-bracket California resident gets the full double exemption only on a slice of that portfolio that happens to be California issues, for example. VTEB captures the full federal exemption, but not the full state value. For top bracket residents of high tax states, the math would imply that a single state fund would deliver a higher realized TEY. For investors in low/no income states, a national fund like VTEB offers you pure upside with no after tax penalty.

Where the TEY begins to break down in the 32% tax bracket and below. At 32% federal plus 3.8% NIIT (35.8% effective), TEY = 3.46% / 0.642 = 5.39%. That is barely a wash with IG corporates and worse than a long Treasury after duration adjustment. Drop to the 24% bracket and the TEY collapses to 4.55%, which is below Treasuries. The break-even is roughly the 35% federal bracket, which corresponds to taxable income above ~$250K single / ~$500K joint.

So, who should own VTEB? Specifically, investors in the top two federal brackets (35% and 37%), holding the position in a taxable account, with material taxable bond exposure they want to swap into a more tax-efficient vehicle. For that investor, even with my Hold rating on the asset class, VTEB is the right tool. The 5.85% TEY is real money, the credit quality is high, and the 3 bp expense ratio means almost none of the yield leaks to fees.

Now, who should not own VTEB? Anyone holding it in an IRA, 401(k), or Roth account is leaving money on the table by giving up yield they did not need to give up. Anyone in the 24% bracket or below is paying a tax-efficiency premium for a benefit they barely use. And anyone who is overweight with munis already in their state-specific SMA should think hard about overlapping before adding national exposure.

The Hold rating reflects my view on the asset class and the entry point, not my view on the vehicle. For the right investor, VTEB is the right vehicle. Just not at the right time for an outsized total return.

Risks

If oil pressure pushes core inflation back above 3%, this could deliver a meaningful price depreciation on a 7 year duration fixed income portfolio. Additionally, supply pressure is something to consider as well. Issuance of Muni’s ran about 10% ahead of Q1 2025, and an elevated supply alongside spring/summer seasonal weakness can pressure ratios to a wider margin. A smaller risk to consider (due to how well diversified VTEB is) is credit migration in stressed states, as CA, IL, and NY are running a modest budget deficit, which are manageable, but worth monitoring.

Takeaway

VTEB is a good tool for tax-sensitive investors who want diversified, low cost, and broad market muni exposure. I wouldn’t particularly own any competitor for that need on an index level. VTEB, for what it is, is truly the best in class. Owning the right tool in the wrong rate environment, however, is still unfavorable, and that's what this situation seems to be. I would rather extend out further on the muni curve through a longer-duration alternative if I needed a broad market, tax-exempt income sleeve today.

data.title: Elbit Systems: War Premium Builds, But Capacity Caps The Upside
data.description:
In early March, I discussed the investment case for Elbit Systems (ESLT). As an Israeli defense contractor, the company is set to benefit from a temporary defense budget hike in support of the war. Since then, the stock price has risen 2.5% and briefly traded over $1,000 which would indicate an 8.5% increase in the stock price. While a 2.5% increase does not seem impressive, we note that Elbit Systems is one of very few defense prime names that actually traded up following the war in Iran. In this report, I review the company’s earnings and assess the investment case for Elbit Systems.

Elbit Systems Pre-War Results Are Impressive

In 2025, Elbit Systems revenues rose 16% to $7.9 billion with operating profits growing 34% to $738 million. This indicates margins of 9.3% compared to 8% a year ago. Free cash flow surged from $233 million to $553 million with EPS increasing 46% to $12.75.

The company saw its land domain revenues increase 38% to $2.32 billion with margins expanding from 9% to 11.4%. Intelligence, Surveillance, Target Acquisition and Reconnaissance and Electronic Warfare or ISTAR&EW revenues grew 16% to $1.53 billion with margins increasing from 7.3% to 8.5%. We note that margins were 11.4% in 2023. So, the current margins are still lower. C4I and Cyber sales grew 16% to $913 million but margins fell from 7.8% to 6% as the segment is experiencing mix shifts and is going through an investment phase. Aerospace sales grew 2% to $2.07 billion with stable margins at 7.4%. Elbit USA saw 7% growth in revenues to $1.69 billion with margins increasing from 3.5% to 7.2%.

So, overall we see strength in the land and ISTAR domains, which is not odd given that demand for munitions, rocket artillery, ground systems, counter-drone, sensors and electronic warfare has been increasing. Simultaneously, we note lower C4I & Cyber margins and stable performance in aerospace. So, not all parts of the business are showing convincing growth.

Backlog Continues To Grow But Capacity Is Not Added Fast Enough

In 2025, the backlog grew 24% despite 16% growth in revenues. The backlog now stands at $28.1 billion with over half expected to convert to revenues over the next two years. As backlog growth continues to outpace revenue growth, we note that from backlog perspective there is support for an acceleration in revenue growth. However, I currently deem it unlikely to happen because the revenues are not demand constrained, but capacity constrained. That also means that Elbit Systems is investing to expand capacity, not only in Israel but primarily in Europe. Adding drone and ammunition capacity in Europe seems to be key. The company has a huge backlog, but it generally runs behind on its delivery schedules. That already led to Romania threatening to cancel its Watchkeeper X drone order valued around $430 million and prompted Elbit to start preparing for first deliveries. The order was placed in 2022 with first deliveries slated for 2025, but Elbit cited force majeure related to the war in Gaza. It is not the case that Elbit Systems did not build the systems, but the final steps to prepare for final acceptance were a bottleneck. It is highly likely that the capacity was utilized to speed up deliveries to the domestic market. The Romanian Government granted two requests to delay deliveries, but did not approve a third one. So, the backlog radiates strong demand for Elbit Systems but the company has to expand it capacity to ease bottlenecks in order to not lose orders.

The current war in Iran could also result in additional demand for Elbit’s products. In the near-term, there should be additional demand for consumables and tactical equipment, but we do note that it does not layer flat on prior outlooks. In the longer-term, Elbit Systems could see international demand strengthening further as its products are war-proven.

Elbit Systems Is A Buy At An Elevated Multiple

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Elbit Systems stock is definitely not cheap. However, the current trading levels reflect a growing war premium with 10% sales growth expected coupled to 18% growth in EBITDA and 11% free cash flow growth through 2028 despite increasing capital expenditures. That brings the price target to $1,094.56 indicating 18% upside. That is a trim from my prior $1,197 price target. It is a big cut, but I believe it reflects that Elbit Systems can satisfy some of the opportunities that the war offers in near term sales but not all given that it is capacity constrained. The risk that Elbit faces is not just on its capacity constraints, but also on the war premium holding. So, that is something that investors need to keep in mind.

Margins are expected to improve substantially in 2026, but as capacity comes online the improvement in margins will likely taper somewhat in 2027. However, overall we see strong improvement in EBITDA margins coupled with improving operating cash flow and stable cash flows through 2027 as the business increases capital expenditures. Potentially, the cash collection may have additional upside in 2027 on order placements and ramp up execution. The company is expected to be in a net cash position this year but we do note that this net cash position is driven by the capital raise it executed in 2025.

Conclusion: Elbit Offers Upside If You Think The War Premium Will Hold

Elbit Systems is definitely an expensive stock trading well above the aerospace and defense peer group. Normally, I don’t feel comfortable with companies trading that much ahead of the peer group and also in this case I do not feel fully comfortable with it. However, we note that Elbit Systems is one of the few defense contractors that is in the middle of a conflict zone and for that reason higher multiples can be justified. We note that the company has incremental upside for demand for consumables and tactical equiment in the near-term, with potential longer-term demand for counter-drone and electronic warfare solutions in the long-term from countries in the region and global demand appeal from Elbit's battle-proven product portfolio. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating for Elbit because of that.

Do you think that Elbit Systems is worth the premium? Let me know in the comment section.

data.title: Diodes Incorporated: Why The Rally Is Overdone
data.description:
Diodes Incorporated (DIOD), a supplier of a wide range of semiconductor products, has moved higher for the last four months or so. The ongoing rally has been supported by several factors, including a better-than-expected earnings report and an upbeat update to DIOD’s financial model, which promises much faster growth in the next three years, compared to what DIOD has shown in recent times. However, the rally may soon run out of steam for a number of reasons.

DIOD has outperformed in 2026

A previous article from December 2025 written by myself mentioned how DIOD had returned to growth in FY2025, something expected to continue in the next fiscal or FY2026. These growth prospects got a boost in the second half of 2025 from troubles at a leading competitor, Nexperia, something that could affect the road ahead for DIOD since the latter stood to benefit if the former left a gap in the market others could take advantage of in terms of sales. On the other hand, the Nexperia issue may not necessarily remain a tailwind beyond the short term, let alone indefinitely. The stock was also listless since it had gone basically sideways for months. In the end, I decided to rate DIOD a hold in the article.

The chart above show how the year 2026 has turned out much better than the latter part of 2025. DIOD spent the second half of 2025 going sideways with ups and downs, but the stock has rallied higher in 2026, especially after the release of the Q4 FY2025 report when the stock gained 26.4% the day after. Furthermore, DIOD might actually be in an ascending channel. If all the highs and lows of recent months are connected to form trendlines, as shown in the chart, a channel appears, which is going higher.

This pattern is likely to draw in speculators as it signals higher prices. At the same time, it’s worth mentioning that DIOD is now in overbought territory with an RSI value in the seventies due to the surge in the stock price, especially in the last few days. If we assume the existence of the aforementioned ascending channel, then it is also worth noting how the stock is closing in on the upper bound of this channel.

Furthermore, it’s also worth mentioning that after closing at $83.07 on Friday, DIOD is close to most price targets on Wall Street. In fact, the average of the price targets sits at $82.33, which means DIOD is already trading above what price targets suggest is worth paying for a stock like DIOD. Put all this together and current conditions could by themselves trigger a pullback in the stock.

What caused DIOD to rally after going flat

The earlier chart shows how DIOD spiked higher after the release of the most recent or Q4 FY2025 report. DIOD not only beat earnings expectations with its Q4 FY2025 report, but it also issued an upbeat outlook with the release of financial targets for the next three years or the FY2025-2028 period. For starters, the consensus estimate expected non-GAAP EPS of $0.27, or what DIOD earned in Q4 FY2024, but DIOD reported net income of $15.7M or $0.34 per share on revenue of $391.58M in Q4 FY2025, an increase of 15.4% YoY.

In terms of GAAP, DIOD reported net income attributable to common stockholders of $10.2M or $0.22 per share in Q4 FY2025, up from $0.18 in Q4 FY2024. For all of FY2025, DIOD reported revenue of $1,482.07M, which resulted in GAAP net income attributable to common stockholders of $66.1M or $1.43 per share and non-GAAP net income of $56.68M or $1.22 per share.

The reason why GAAP earnings are higher is because they benefited from a $28.56M unrealized gain on investments and a $13.73M gain on the disposal of a subsidiary. Quarterly guidance calls for revenue of $395M, plus or minus 3 percent, in Q1 FY2026, which represents an increase of 19% YoY at the midpoint. DIOD ended FY2025 with cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments totaling $377M, partially offset of total debt of $56M.

More importantly, DIOD is optimistic about the outlook as it calls for significant growth ahead. DIOD updated its financial model for the next three years or FY2025-2028 by targeting revenue of $2B, gross profit of $700M, gross margin of 35+%, and non-GAAP EPS of $4.00+. In comparison, FY2025 ended with revenue of $1,482.07M, gross profit of $462.44M, gross margin of 31.2% and non-GAAP EPS of $1.22.

Why DIOD is arguably overvalued

However, while DIOD expects strong growth in the next three years, it seems all this growth has already been priced in at current valuations and then some after the rally of the last several months. For instance, TTM non-GAAP EPS totaled $1.22, which accords DIOD a P/E ratio of 68.09x with the stock last closing at $83.07.

The issue here is that all this points to DIOD being overvalued. According to say the methodology favored by Peter Lynch, DIOD needs to grow earnings at a CAGR of 68.09% in the next 3-5 years in order to be deemed fairly valued relative to growth. Yet according to DIOD itself, earnings are only expected to grow from $1.22 in FY2025 to $4.00+ in FY2028, which represents a CAGR of 48.56% in FY2025-2028.

In other words, DIOD is now overvalued relative to growth after the recent surge in the stock price. DIOD left some headroom by calling for $4.00+, or somewhat above $4.00, but if DIOD is to grow earnings at a CAGR of 68.09%, FY2028 non-GAAP EPS would need to reach $5.79, or well in excess of what DIOD seems to believe can be realistically achieved given the financial model.

It’s worth putting expected growth into proper context

It’s also important to put the growth over the next three years into proper context. While growing revenue at a CAGR of 10.5% and non-GAAP EPS at a CAGR of 48.6% in FY2025-2028 may seem impressive, they start to look much less so when taking into consideration that this expected growth was preceded by a downturn that caused revenue to contract by 9.5%, on average, per year in the past three years. Similarly, earnings contracted by 41.1% per year during this time.

Even if DIOD goes on to hit all the targets laid out in the financial model in FY2028, DIOD would still not be where it was in FY2022, or three years ago. That is because DIOD posted revenue of $2.0B, or exactly what DIOD is aiming for in FY2028, but the bottom line was much better with non-GAAP EPS of $7.36, thanks to a gross margin of 41.3% and gross profit of $827.2M. The last three are much better than what DIOD hopes to achieve three years from now. Revenue would have grown at a CAGR of zero percent because FY2022 revenue of $2.0B is the same as the aimed for revenue of $2.0B in FY2028.

In other words, DIOD will need three years just to get back where it was three years ago in terms of the top line and in terms of the bottom line it will still not be anywhere close. By FY2028 DIOD will have spent six years not making any real progress in terms of revenue and it will have regressed in terms of net profits. So while the next three years may look impressive in terms of the headline growth numbers, an argument can be made that DIOD is not actually doing that great a job in terms of growth.

How problems at Nexperia could lead to more competition for DIOD in the future

DIOD mentions Nexperia as one of its leading competitors in its latest Form 10-K. Nexperia is particularly strong in the automotive market, which is a market DIOD has focused on in recent times. For instance, DIOD introduced 650 new part numbers in 2025, which includes an outsized proportion of about 40% for the automotive market.

This suggests that DIOD could benefit from recent problems at Nexperia, which erupted in the fall of 2025 and which have caused disruptions because the company is essentially split into two, Nexperia based in Europe and Nexperia based in China. In fact, there are already indications DIOD is benefiting because the automotive segment grew by 24% in Q4 FY2025 and 20% for all of FY2025, both well ahead of overall growth at 15% and 13%, respectively.

However, there are increasing signs there could be two completely separate Nexperia’s in the future. Before Dutch authorities seized control of Nexperia’s European assets from its Chinese owner in China, the European part of Nexperia was mainly responsible for producing the actual chips on wafers, which would then be send to China, and Nexperia’s Chinese facilities to be more exact, for further processing into final form, which includes the packaging.

But Nexperia China has recently announced that it has started producing its own chips using 12-inch wafers. In theory, this means that Nexperia China no longer has to rely on sourcing wafers from Nexperia Europe. The 12-inch form factor should also incur a number of advantages like lower cost compared to the 6 and 8-inch wafers used by Nexperia Europe, although it will take time as depreciation has to be accounted for in the short term.

If Nexperia Europe proceeds to come up with its own substitute for China, then it’s possible we might end up with a situation where DIOD will not only have to compete against one Nexperia, but two. DIOD is primarily known for its discrete chips, hence the name Diodes, but this market is rather fragmented with lots of competition. Adding more competition to the table will only make it harder for everyone.

Investor takeaways

Betting on long DIOD has been a winning play in the last four months or so. The stock has already gained 68.4% YTD and the first half is not even over. Most of the gains came after DIOD soundly beat expectations with its Q4 FY2025 report and after DIOD laid out the prospect of strong growth in the next three years.

However, while it is possible the momentum followers will push the stock price higher, there is an argument to be made that the right move is to refrain from jumping in. This is because even if DIOD hits the targets laid out over the next three years, DIOD can be deemed overvalued relative to growth for the next three years, as elaborated on in greater detail earlier in the article.

If DIOD is to warrant current valuations, which have jumped higher after the recent rally in the stock, DIOD would have to far surpass what DIOD itself seems to think is realistic, assuming that the targets laid out by DIOD represents what it believes can be achieved in the next three years. This looks implausible. The more plausible outcome is that growth comes in as DIOD aims for. If this happens, then DIOD is not a buy.

Furthermore, even if growth comes in as aimed for in the next three years, it would only partially make up the losses of the past three years, especially when it comes to earnings. If someone, for instance, got in on DIOD in 2022, he or she would still not be in 2028 where they were in 2022, even though six years have passed, and that is assuming there are no hiccups that somehow cause DIOD to miss its targets.

It’s also worth mentioning that the stock is overbought and buying a stock that has gone up as much as DIOD has in a relatively short amount of time is risky because of the possibility that the stock is increasingly overdue for a move lower. This does not mean higher prices are not possible, but the risk buyers get a haircut is there.

I am thus neutral on DIOD. It may be temping to jump in with the way the stock has rallied higher, but valuations matter in the long run and there is every reason to believe that the asking price is too high after the gains of 2026. It may not happen right now, but as things stand, the risk for DIOD is very much to the downside.
